title: Topic 261: The Psychology of Sequels: Familiarity vs. Innovation
content:
# Topic 261: The Psychology of Sequels: Familiarity vs. Innovation

## ABSTRACT

This paper courageously delves into the paradoxical world of sequels, a realm where Hollywood and the gaming industry have discovered the closest thing to a perpetual motion machine. We explore the psychological underpinnings of our collective obsession with the familiar, from the comforting embrace of the mere-exposure effect to the siren song of nostalgia. With the intellectual rigor of a sleep-deprived graduate student and the comedic timing of a slightly-less-sleep-deprived stand-up comedian, we dissect the delicate dance between innovation and familiarity. Why do we flock to see *Rocky XII: Still Punching*? Why do we preorder the eighth installment of a game that is essentially the same as the seventh, but with better graphics for the horse armor? This paper will answer these questions, and more, while simultaneously justifying the author's extensive knowledge of video game lore as a legitimate academic pursuit.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand casino of entertainment, sequels are the house's safest bet. They are the cinematic and interactive equivalent of comfort food, a warm, predictable bowl of macaroni and cheese in a world of intimidating, experimental gastronomy. The modern entertainment landscape is a veritable sequel-pocalypse, a testament to the fact that we, as consumers, are often more interested in the devil we know than the angel we don't. This paper will explore the psychological drivers behind this phenomenon, examining why the familiar is so often more appealing than the novel, and how the entertainment industry has masterfully exploited this quirk of human cognition.

We will embark on a journey through the hallowed halls of psychology, exploring concepts such as the mere-exposure effect, cognitive fluency, and the powerful pull of nostalgia. We will also venture into the treacherous territory of the innovation-familiarity trade-off, a tightrope walk that has led to both triumphant successes and spectacular failures. For every *The Godfather Part II*, there is a *Godfather Part III*, a cautionary tale whispered in hushed tones in the corridors of film schools. For every *Mass Effect 2*, there is a *Deus Ex: Invisible War*, a game that dared to innovate and was promptly punished for it.

This paper is not merely an academic exercise; it is a quest for understanding. It is an attempt to unravel the enigma of our own consumer behavior, to understand why we are so willing to part with our hard-earned cash for another slice of the same pie. Is it a sign of our collective creative bankruptcy, or is it a rational response to an overwhelming sea of choice? Are we simply creatures of habit, or are we savvy consumers making a calculated bet on a known quantity? With a healthy dose of humor and a dash of academic pomposity, we will attempt to find out.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of sequels. While it may not be the sexiest of topics, the psychology of consumer choice and the power of familiarity have been extensively studied. The cornerstone of our understanding lies in the **mere-exposure effect**, a concept first explored in depth by Robert Zajonc in the 1960s. Zajonc, in a series of experiments that presumably involved showing people a lot of the same thing over and over again, demonstrated that we tend to develop a preference for things simply because we are familiar with them. This is why that song you hated on the radio is now your go-to karaoke jam, and why you are more likely to buy the brand of toothpaste your parents used.

Building on this, the concept of **cognitive fluency** suggests that familiar stimuli are easier for our brains to process. This ease of processing is inherently pleasurable, leading to a positive affective response. In a world where we are constantly bombarded with new information, the familiar offers a welcome respite, a cognitive vacation for our overworked neurons. This is why, after a long day of making difficult decisions, you are more likely to rewatch an old episode of *Friends* than to start a new, critically acclaimed but mentally demanding series.

Of course, we cannot discuss the appeal of sequels without mentioning the potent, rose-tinted drug that is **nostalgia**. As documented by researchers like Constantine Sedikides, nostalgia is not just a wistful longing for the past; it is a powerful emotion that can increase feelings of social connectedness, meaning, and well-being. Sequels, by their very nature, are nostalgia-delivery systems, allowing us to revisit beloved characters and worlds. They are a way of recapturing the magic of our youth, a time when our biggest worry was whether we would have enough quarters for the arcade.

However, the literature also warns of the dangers of excessive familiarity. The **wear-out effect**, a concept well-known in advertising research, suggests that after a certain point, repeated exposure to a stimulus can lead to boredom and even annoyance. This is the point at which a beloved franchise becomes a tired cash-grab, a pale imitation of its former self. The challenge for creators, then, is to find the sweet spot between the comforting embrace of the familiar and the exciting allure of the new. This delicate balance, or lack thereof, is what separates the legendary sequels from the ones we would all rather forget.

## ANALYSIS

The billion-dollar question for every studio executive and game developer is: how much innovation is too much? The answer, it seems, lies in a nuanced understanding of the psychology of the consumer. We are, at our core, creatures of habit, but we are also novelty-seeking beings. This inherent contradiction is the tightrope that every sequel must walk.

The most successful sequels are those that manage to thread the needle, offering an experience that is both familiar and fresh. They are the ones that understand that we want to see our old friends, but we want to see them do new things. *The Dark Knight* is a masterclass in this regard. It brought back the beloved characters and world of *Batman Begins*, but it also introduced a new, terrifyingly unpredictable villain in the Joker. The film was a commercial and critical success precisely because it respected the source material while also pushing the boundaries of the superhero genre.

In the world of gaming, the *Uncharted* series is another prime example. Each installment features the same charismatic protagonist, Nathan Drake, and the same blend of platforming, puzzle-solving, and third-person shooting. However, each game also takes us to new, exotic locations, introduces new characters, and raises the stakes in new and exciting ways. The core experience is familiar, but the details are constantly changing, keeping the player engaged and eager for more.

On the other hand, we have the cautionary tales, the sequels that strayed too far from the path and paid the price. *Deus Ex: Invisible War* is a classic example. The original *Deus Ex* was a complex, challenging RPG with a deep, branching narrative. The sequel, in an attempt to appeal to a wider audience, streamlined many of these elements, simplifying the gameplay and consolidating the story. The result was a game that alienated the hardcore fans of the original without being compelling enough to attract a new audience. It was a sequel that forgot what made the original so special in the first place.

Then there are the franchises that have, arguably, become too comfortable, too reliant on the power of familiarity. The *Call of Duty* series, while still a commercial juggernaut, has been criticized for its lack of innovation. Each new installment offers a slightly different flavor of the same core experience, a fast-paced, multiplayer shooter with a bombastic single-player campaign. While this formula has proven to be incredibly successful, it has also led to a sense of fatigue among some players, a feeling that they have played this game before, because, in a way, they have.

Ultimately, the success of a sequel hinges on its ability to manage expectations. We come to a sequel with a pre-existing set of expectations, a mental model of what the experience should be. The best sequels are those that both meet and subvert these expectations, giving us what we want while also surprising us with something we didn't know we wanted. It is a delicate and often thankless task, but when it is done right, the results can be truly magical.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The history of entertainment is littered with the corpses of failed sequels and the triumphant monuments of successful ones. Let us take a moment to conduct a brief, and entirely unscientific, post-mortem on a few notable cases.

**Case Study 1: *The Godfather* vs. *The Godfather Part II***

*The Godfather* is a cinematic masterpiece, a film that needs no introduction. Its sequel, *The Godfather Part II*, is arguably even better. How did it achieve this seemingly impossible feat? By doing something incredibly clever: it told two stories at once. It continued the story of Michael Corleone's descent into darkness, while also flashing back to the story of his father, Vito Corleone's, rise to power. This dual-narrative structure was a brilliant innovation, allowing the film to both expand on the original story and to provide a new, compelling narrative. It was a sequel that was both a continuation and a prequel, a familiar story told in a new and exciting way.

**Case Study 2: *Speed* vs. *Speed 2: Cruise Control***

*Speed* was a high-concept action film that was as simple as it was brilliant: a bus that can't slow down. It was a tense, thrilling ride from start to finish. The sequel, *Speed 2: Cruise Control*, made a fatal error: it took the bus out of the equation. Instead of a bus, we had a cruise ship. A very, very slow cruise ship. The sense of speed, the very thing that made the original so exciting, was gone. It was a sequel that fundamentally misunderstood what made the original work, a cautionary tale about the dangers of changing the formula too much.

**Case Study 3: The *Legend of Zelda* series**

If there is one franchise that has mastered the art of the sequel, it is *The Legend of Zelda*. For over three decades, Nintendo has been releasing new *Zelda* games, each one a new adventure for the heroic Link. The core elements are always the same: a vast world to explore, dungeons to conquer, and a princess to save. However, each game also introduces new mechanics, new items, and a new art style. From the cel-shaded visuals of *The Wind Waker* to the open-world freedom of *Breath of the Wild*, the *Zelda* series is a masterclass in how to keep a franchise fresh and exciting for generations of players. It is a testament to the power of innovation within a familiar framework, a series that is always the same, and always different.

## DISCUSSION

What, then, can we learn from our collective obsession with sequels? What does it say about us as consumers, and as human beings? On the one hand, it speaks to our innate desire for comfort and security. In a world of overwhelming choice, the familiar offers a safe harbor, a predictable and reliable source of enjoyment. There is a certain comfort in knowing what you are going to get, in returning to a world and characters that you already know and love.

However, our love of sequels also reveals a certain tension in our consumer habits. We crave the familiar, but we also yearn for the new. We want to be comforted, but we also want to be surprised. This is the paradox that lies at the heart of the sequel phenomenon, the delicate dance between nostalgia and novelty. The most successful sequels are those that understand this paradox, that give us a taste of the old while also offering a glimpse of the new.

Furthermore, the success of sequels has profound implications for the entertainment industry as a whole. It has created a blockbuster mentality, a focus on big-budget, franchise-driven films and games that are designed to appeal to the widest possible audience. This has led to a certain homogenization of the cultural landscape, a world in which original, standalone stories can struggle to find an audience. It is a world in which risk is a dirty word, and familiarity is king.

But is this necessarily a bad thing? While it is easy to bemoan the lack of originality in modern entertainment, it is also worth remembering that sequels can, at their best, be a force for good. They can allow for deeper, more nuanced storytelling, for the exploration of characters and themes over multiple installments. They can build on what has come before, creating a rich and rewarding experience for the dedicated fan. And, let's be honest, sometimes we just want to see a big robot punch a big monster. And there is nothing wrong with that.

## CONCLUSION

In the final analysis, our relationship with sequels is a complex and often contradictory one. We are drawn to the comforting glow of the familiar, yet we are also easily bored by a lack of novelty. We are, in essence, the Goldilocks of the entertainment world, constantly searching for a sequel that is “just right.” This paper has attempted to shed some light on this curious psychological dance, to understand why we are so willing to line up for the eighth installment of a franchise that, by all rights, should have ended at the third. The truth is, sequels are more than just a cynical cash-grab; they are a reflection of our own deeply human desires for comfort, for nostalgia, and for the occasional, mind-blowing explosion. So, the next time you find yourself pre-ordering the latest installment of your favorite game, or eagerly awaiting the next superhero blockbuster, do not despair. You are not a mindless consumer; you are a complex psychological being, engaged in a delicate and fascinating dance with the forces of familiarity and innovation. Or, you just really, really like seeing things blow up. Either way, it’s perfectly normal.
references:
Loock, K. (2017). The sequel paradox: Repetition, innovation, and hollywood's hit film formula. Film Studies, 17(1), 92-108.
Sedikides, C., Wildschut, T., Arndt, J., & Routledge, C. (2008). Nostalgia: Past, present, and future. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 17(5), 304–307.
Shu-Yu, Y. (2013). When do original movies dominate sequels? The moderating effects of film types and product knowledge. Asia Pacific Management Review, 18(3), 285-300.
The Decision Lab. (n.d.). Mere Exposure Effect. The Decision Lab. Retrieved from https://thedecisionlab.com/biases/mere-exposure-effect
Tomkinson, S. (2020). Video games through the refrain: Innovation and familiarity. Journal of Gaming & Virtual Worlds, 12(1), 25-41.
Zajonc, R. B. (1968). Attitudinal effects of mere exposure. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 9(2, Pt.2), 1–27.
